text: Polarized training produces the greatest increase in VO2peak among well-trained endurance athletes compared to high-volume, threshold, or high-intensity interval training over a 9-week period.
Polarized training increases time to exhaustion during incremental ramp protocols more than threshold, high-volume, or HIIT approaches.
Polarized training increases peak velocity/power output more than other training intensity distributions over 9 weeks.
Both polarized training and HIIT improve velocity/power at the 4 mmol·L⁻¹ lactate threshold, while high-volume and threshold training do not.
High-intensity interval training reduces body mass in well-trained endurance athletes while high-volume, threshold, and polarized training do not.
Threshold-only and high-volume-only training do not produce meaningful improvements in VO2peak, time to exhaustion, or peak power in already well-trained endurance athletes over 9 weeks.
